content.text: An exception and waiver process is the formal mechanism through which engineering teams request and receive permission to deviate from established architecture standards. Every mature Design Review Board (DRB) maintains a published set of standards: approved AI model providers, required security controls, data residency rules, latency SLOs, and so on. When a project cannot comply with one of these standards, it does not simply proceed unchecked — it submits an exception request that documents the deviation, its justification, the risks it creates, and the compensating controls that will mitigate those risks.

In agentic AI systems, the need for exceptions arises more frequently than in conventional software. Agentic architectures are evolving rapidly: a foundation model released six months ago may offer a capability that the current standard does not anticipate; a real-time autonomous agent may need sub-100 ms end-to-end latency that is inconsistent with mandatory audit logging; an experimental feature may require a third-party tool integration not yet on the approved vendor list. The exception process is not a loophole — it is a structured safety valve that allows innovation to proceed under controlled, time-bounded, and traceable conditions.
The process spans three artifacts: the
(the living record of all active exceptions), and the

(the state machine that governs how an exception moves from submission to expiry or revocation). Together they give the DRB visibility into every active deviation and the ability to detect when a pattern of exceptions signals that a standard needs updating rather than continued waiver.

Without a formal exception process, architecture standards decay silently. Teams under time-to-market pressure make one-off decisions to skip a required control, ship with an unapproved provider, or defer a security review. Each decision lives only in a Slack thread or a ticket comment. The DRB has no visibility. Risk accumulates invisibly, and when an incident occurs, the investigation discovers that three teams had independently made the same undocumented deviation for months.

A concrete example in agentic AI: an organisation's standard mandates that all LLM API calls be routed through a corporate API gateway for rate limiting, cost attribution, and PII scrubbing. A new team building a low-latency voice agent finds that gateway adds 80 ms of overhead, pushing end-to-end latency above their SLO. Without an exception process, they quietly bypass the gateway. Six months later, during a data audit, the security team discovers that raw user voice transcripts — containing names and account numbers — are being sent directly to the model provider with no PII scrubbing in the path. The exception process would have surfaced this risk at submission time, required a compensating control (client-side PII redaction), and time-boxed the deviation to 90 days pending a gateway performance improvement.

The secondary benefit is pattern detection. If the DRB receives five separate exception requests in one quarter all citing the same standard as impractical, that is not five teams making poor decisions — it is a signal that the standard itself is out of step with current practice. The exception registry makes this pattern visible so the DRB can update the standard rather than approve an unlimited stream of waivers.
Every deviation from standard is documented with its risk assessment and compensating controls. The DRB and security teams can see exactly where the risk posture deviates from policy at any time.
Exceptions carry a mandatory sunset date, preventing indefinite accumulation of technical debt. The organisation does not end up running on ten-year-old waivers that nobody owns.
Each exception is linked to the approving DRB authority, the business justification, and the project that requested it. Audit trails are complete without manual reconstruction.
Teams can move fast on experimental agentic features without violating policy, because the exception process is the legitimate path for justified deviations rather than a reason to block progress.
Recurring patterns in the exception registry drive evidence-based updates to architecture standards rather than relying on committee intuition.
Exception requestors commit to compensating controls and sunset plans. The owning team is named in the registry and receives automated reminders before expiry.

Submitting, reviewing, and tracking exceptions takes time. If the DRB does not right-size the process (e.g. fast-track for low-risk deviations), it can become a bottleneck that incentivises teams to avoid it entirely.

An exception registry that is not actively maintained — with no automated expiry alerts or ownership reassignments — quickly becomes inaccurate. Stale data is worse than no data, because it creates false confidence.
If approval rates are very high, teams learn to seek exceptions routinely rather than investing in compliant solutions. The exception process should be genuinely selective, not a rubber stamp.
A sunset date is only useful if it is enforced. Without automated tracking and escalation, exceptions routinely expire on paper but persist in production for years.

If the engineer who submitted the exception leaves the organisation, institutional knowledge of the compensating controls and the rationale for the deviation can be lost. Documentation quality in the request matters enormously.

The exception process begins when a project team identifies that compliance with a specific architecture standard is infeasible or disproportionately costly for their use case. Common triggers in agentic AI systems include: time-to-market pressure that cannot wait for a standard to be formally updated; a unique capability requirement such as a newly released model not yet on the approved provider list; or an experimental feature that is explicitly not production-ready under the current policy but needs to be shipped to validate a hypothesis.

that follows the DRB's published template. The request identifies the specific standard being waived, provides a business justification with measurable rationale, includes a risk assessment that enumerates what could go wrong, proposes compensating controls that reduce the residual risk to an acceptable level, and commits to a sunset date by which the team will either achieve compliance or submit a renewal request. The request is submitted as a Jira issue or ServiceNow request item and enters the DRB review queue.

The DRB reviews the request at the appropriate authority level based on the severity of the deviation. Low-risk exceptions (e.g. a minor version pin to an approved library) may be approved by a single principal engineer. High-risk exceptions (e.g. bypassing PII controls for a production system) require a full DRB quorum and a security architect sign-off. The outcome — approved, approved with modifications, or rejected — is recorded in the exception registry along with the approver, the date, and any conditions attached to the approval.
Once approved, the exception transitions to

and monitoring begins. Automated tooling (integrated with Jira or ServiceNow) sends reminders to the owning team 30 days before the sunset date. If the team resolves the underlying issue, they close the exception and mark it Expired. If they need more time, they submit a renewal before the sunset date. The DRB can also Revoke an exception at any time if circumstances change — for example, if a compensating control is found to be ineffective, or if the risk profile escalates due to a change in the system's data classification.

attrs.alt: Exception lifecycle state machine: Submitted flows to Under Review via DRB intake, which branches to Approved (green) or Rejected (red). Approved leads to Active (amber), which transitions to Expired on sunset date or Revoked (purple dashed) on policy violation.
attrs.title: Exception Lifecycle State Machine

content.text: The diagram shows the seven states an exception moves through during its lifetime.
is the entry state when a waiver request is filed.
begins when the DRB intake queue picks it up for evaluation. The review branches to
(red). An approved exception is activated and enters the
state where it is in force and monitored. From Active, two exits are possible:
(early termination due to policy violation or risk escalation, shown as a dashed purple path).

The exception and waiver process applies whenever a project cannot meet a specific DRB-published architecture standard and chooses to proceed anyway rather than delay until the standard can be changed through the normal DRB update cycle. Common scenarios in agentic AI systems:

A competitive product launch requires shipping in six weeks, but the approved provider list does not yet include a newly released model that provides a critical capability. An exception allows the team to ship with the new provider while the DRB fast-tracks a formal evaluation.

A multimodal agent needs a vision-language model that has no equivalent on the approved list. The team cannot simply substitute an approved model because the capability does not exist there. An exception documents the gap and sets a deadline for the DRB to either add the capability to the standard or formalise the deviation.

A feature is explicitly in a beta or experimental phase. Standard controls designed for production systems — extensive audit logging, full DR failover — would be disproportionate. An exception scopes the deviation to the experimental lifecycle and automatically expires when the feature graduates to production or is discontinued.

A mandatory audit logging standard adds 120 ms to each LLM call. A real-time agentic system with a 200 ms end-to-end budget cannot absorb this overhead. An exception grants a temporary waiver while an asynchronous logging path is engineered.
Do not use the exception process
as a substitute for standard updates. If the same standard is being excepted repeatedly, the correct action is to open a DRB standard change proposal, not accumulate a backlog of individual waivers.

Publish the DRB exception policy in your internal architecture wiki. Define what requires an exception, which DRB authority level approves each risk tier, and what the maximum allowed sunset period is (e.g. 90 days for high-risk, 180 days for medium-risk).

Create a waiver request template. The template should capture: exception ID, requesting team, standard reference, summary of deviation, business justification, risk assessment (likelihood and impact), compensating controls, sunset date, and rollback plan.

Build the exception registry. A Jira project or ServiceNow catalog item works well. The registry must be queryable by standard, team, state, and sunset date. Export to a JSON or CSV for monthly DRB review.

Automate lifecycle management. Configure Jira automation or ServiceNow workflow to send reminders 30 days before sunset, escalate to the DRB if renewal is not submitted 7 days before expiry, and auto-close exceptions past their sunset date with a state of Expired.

Run a monthly exception pattern review. Produce a report of all active exceptions grouped by standard. Any standard with three or more active exceptions in a rolling 90-day window triggers a standard update proposal.

Integrate exception status into CI/CD. A build pipeline can query the exception registry API and fail the build if a project references an unapproved deviation (i.e. one that is not covered by an Active exception).
Below is a JSON schema for the exception request object that should be enforced at submission time. Validating the schema on intake prevents incomplete requests from entering the DRB review queue.

{
  "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema",
  "$id": "https://drb.example.com/schemas/exception-request.json",
  "title": "ExceptionRequest",
  "description": "Waiver request submitted to the Design Review Board for an architecture standard exception.",
  "type": "object",
  "required": [
    "id",
    "requestedBy",
    "project",
    "standardRef",
    "deviationSummary",
    "businessJustification",
    "riskAssessment",
    "compensatingControls",
    "sunsetDate",
    "rollbackPlan"
  ],
  "properties": {
    "id": {
      "type": "string",
      "pattern": "^EXC-[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{4}$",
      "description": "Unique exception identifier, format EXC-YYYY-NNNN."
    },
    "requestedBy": {
      "type": "object",
      "required": ["name", "email", "team"],
      "properties": {
        "name":  { "type": "string" },
        "email": { "type": "string", "format": "email" },
# ... (truncated)
Jira Automation for Sunset Reminders (Python / Jira REST API)

# exception_monitor.py
# Run daily via cron or a scheduled GitHub Actions workflow.
# Queries the exception registry and sends reminders / escalations.

import datetime
import json
import os
import smtplib
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
from typing import Any

import requests

JIRA_BASE = os.environ["JIRA_BASE_URL"]          # e.g. https://jira.example.com
JIRA_TOKEN = os.environ["JIRA_API_TOKEN"]
JIRA_PROJECT = "EXC"                             # exception registry project key
SMTP_HOST = os.environ.get("SMTP_HOST", "localhost")


def fetch_active_exceptions() -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
    """Return all Jira issues in the EXC project with state = Active."""
    jql = f'project = {JIRA_PROJECT} AND status = "Active" ORDER BY "Sunset Date" ASC'
    resp = requests.get(
        f"{JIRA_BASE}/rest/api/3/search",
        params={"jql": jql, "fields": "summary,assignee,customfield_10100,customfield_10101"},
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {JIRA_TOKEN}"},
        timeout=10,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["issues"]
# ... (truncated)

A financial services firm operates a DRB that governs twelve agentic AI systems in production. Their architecture standard AI-STD-009 mandates that all agentic workflows using tool calls must implement a human-in-the-loop confirmation step before executing any action with real financial impact (fund transfers, trades, account changes). This standard was written when agents were unreliable, but by mid-2026 the firm's autonomous credit analysis agent has a 99.4% accuracy rate verified over 18 months of shadow operation.

The credit team submits exception EXC-2026-0018 requesting to waive the mandatory HITL confirmation for credit decisions below $25,000 where the agent's confidence score exceeds 0.95. Their business justification: the current HITL step adds 4–8 hours to credit decisions, causing the firm to lose small business customers to competitors who approve in under 30 minutes. Compensating controls include: a real-time anomaly detector that flags unusual decision clusters for retrospective human review, a $25,000 per-customer daily exposure cap enforced in the system, and a weekly audit report reviewed by the Chief Risk Officer.

The DRB approves the exception at Level 3 authority (CTO sign-off required due to financial risk) with a 90-day sunset. After 60 days, the monitoring data shows no anomalous decisions and the anomaly detector has had zero false negatives. The team files a standard change proposal to update AI-STD-009 to formally permit autonomous decisions below $25,000 with documented accuracy thresholds — exactly the pattern the exception process is designed to surface.

The most common failure in exception management is approving exceptions with sunset dates that are never enforced. A registry full of Active exceptions with past sunset dates is worse than no process at all: it creates false confidence that deviations are time-bounded while they persist indefinitely in production. Sunset enforcement must be automated from day one. Manual calendar reminders are insufficient — teams change, people leave, reminders get missed. The automation in the implementation example above is the minimum viable approach.
Compensating Controls That Do Not Compensate

A compensating control that reads "the team will follow best practices" or "additional testing will be performed" is not a control — it is an intention. Compensating controls must be specific, implemented (with an implementation date), and verifiable. "Client-side PII redaction using Presidio applied before every API call, covering 18 entity types" is a control. "We will be careful" is not. The DRB should reject requests whose compensating controls cannot be independently verified.
Using Exceptions to Avoid Standard Updates

When a team submits its third renewal of the same exception, it is a strong signal that the standard is wrong, not that the team keeps failing to comply. DRBs that approve renewals without asking whether the standard should be updated are deferring a governance problem rather than solving it. Track renewal counts in the registry and treat any exception reaching its second renewal as an automatic trigger for a standard change proposal.

Senior architects sometimes give informal verbal approval for a deviation in a Slack channel or a meeting. The team proceeds without filing a formal exception request, and the deviation is never entered in the registry. This defeats the entire purpose of the process. The DRB must communicate clearly that informal approvals have no standing — any deviation not in the exception registry is non-compliant, regardless of what was said in a conversation.
Right-size the process by risk tier.

Low-risk exceptions (e.g. using a library version one patch behind the approved pin) should require a single reviewer and complete in under 24 hours. Reserve full DRB quorum for high-risk deviations. Uniform heavyweight review for all exceptions creates a bottleneck that drives non-compliance.
Make the template a living document.

The waiver request template should evolve as the DRB learns what information is actually needed for good decisions. Review and update it quarterly based on exceptions that turned out to be poorly understood or whose controls failed.

Making the registry read-accessible to all engineers (not just the DRB) creates accountability and allows teams to discover that another team already has an approved exception for the same deviation they are considering. This reduces duplicate submissions and enables exceptions to evolve into candidate standards.
Treat exceptions as a learning signal.

Every exception is a data point about where the architecture standards are misaligned with engineering reality. A DRB that reviews exception patterns quarterly and uses them to drive standard updates is performing governance correctly.

Pipeline checks that query the exception registry and fail builds referencing non-approved deviations close the enforcement loop. Without this, the exception process only governs at review time, not at deployment time.

A rejected exception request must explain why it was rejected in enough detail for the team to understand either how to redesign their approach or how to submit a stronger case. Bare rejections teach teams nothing and encourage resubmission of the same weak request.

As an organisation's agentic AI portfolio grows, the exception registry must scale. A small organisation with 5 AI systems may manage exceptions in a spreadsheet. An enterprise with 50 production agentic systems will generate dozens of exception requests per month and needs a queryable, API-driven registry with dashboards. The tooling choices here are essentially the same as for any process management: use the same system (Jira, ServiceNow) already used for other governance workflows so that integrations, alerting, and reporting infrastructure can be reused.

The CI/CD integration that queries the registry at build time must be fast. Cache the registry state (e.g. in a JSON artifact) and refresh it on a short interval (15 minutes) rather than querying the live system on every pipeline run.

Monthly DRB pattern reviews require aggregated reports: exceptions by standard, by team, by state, by risk tier, and by age. Build these as scheduled exports from the registry rather than manual queries. Grafana or Datadog dashboards fed from a Jira export are practical and low-maintenance.

Expired and revoked exceptions are audit evidence. They must be retained in read-only form for at least the organisation's standard records retention period (typically 3–7 years depending on regulatory context). Archive them to object storage rather than deleting from the active registry, keeping the active registry fast and uncluttered.

The most common exception registry platform in engineering organisations. Custom issue types, custom fields (sunset date, risk tier, standard ref), and Jira Automation rules for reminders and state transitions make it a workable registry without custom development. The REST API allows CI/CD integration and programmatic reporting.

Preferred in heavily regulated industries (finance, healthcare). Provides audit-grade record management, workflow engine, and out-of-the-box GRC (Governance, Risk, Compliance) module that can model the exception lifecycle natively. Higher setup cost than Jira but stronger compliance posture.

The open-source PII detection and anonymisation library used as a compensating control in many agentic AI exceptions. When the exception is "bypassing PII scrubbing at the gateway", Presidio applied client-side is the standard compensating control.

Can be used to enforce exception-aware policies in CI/CD pipelines. A bundle containing the current exception registry state allows OPA policies to evaluate whether a given deployment configuration is covered by an active exception before allowing the deployment to proceed.
Spotify's developer portal can be extended with a TechDocs page that surfaces a team's active exceptions alongside their service catalog entry, giving developers a unified view of compliance status.

The formal exception process sits between the high rigor of a standard change proposal (which permanently updates policy for everyone) and the zero governance of informal approval. It is the correct choice when a deviation is justified for a specific project and timeframe but not ready for universal adoption. Standard change proposals are better when the deviation is likely correct for most teams and the goal is to update the standard. Informal approvals and ticket comments should never be used for deviations that carry security, privacy, or financial risk.

The exception registry is itself a security asset. It documents every known deviation from the architecture standards, including security controls. Access to this registry should be read-wide (all engineers can see active exceptions) but write-restricted (only DRB members can create or modify exception records). Tampering with exception records — particularly changing sunset dates or approval status — must generate an immutable audit log entry.

Every state transition in the exception lifecycle must be logged with a timestamp and the identity of the actor who triggered the transition. This log must be append-only and not editable by the actors it records.

Stated compensating controls must be verified before an exception is moved from Approved to Active. A DRB member or designated reviewer should confirm that the control is actually deployed, not just planned.

When a security incident occurs, the first query should be whether any active exceptions cover the affected system. If an incident traces to an excepted deviation, the exception must be immediately revoked and the root cause analysis must evaluate whether the compensating controls failed or were never adequate.

A renewal request should require a fresh risk assessment, not a copy of the original. If the system's data classification has changed, if the threat landscape has evolved, or if the compensating control has shown gaps, the renewal review must reflect these changes.
Think of architecture standards as a
and the exception process as a

. Building codes exist because they encode hard-won lessons about what makes structures safe and habitable. But codes are written for the common case: a historic building renovation may require structural deviations that comply with the spirit of the code while departing from its letter. A variance permit grants a specific building, at a specific address, permission to deviate in a specific and documented way, with an inspector's verification that the alternative approach is safe.

The DRB is the building inspector. The exception request is the variance application. The compensating controls are the alternative safety measures the architect proposes in lieu of code compliance. The sunset date is the permit expiry — the permit does not grant permanent permission, only time-bounded permission, because the underlying code may be updated in the interim or the conditions that justified the variance may change. The exception registry is the county records office: a public, permanent, queryable record of every variance ever granted.

The analogy extends to enforcement: a building that operates past its permit expiry without renewal is not just non-compliant on paper — it may be structurally unsafe in ways that are not yet visible. An agentic AI system running on an expired exception carries the same invisible risk: a compensating control may have degraded, the threat landscape may have shifted, or the business context that justified the deviation may no longer exist.
The exception and waiver process is the legitimate path for justified architecture deviations in agentic AI systems — it enables innovation under governance rather than blocking it or ignoring it.

A waiver request must include a business justification with measurable rationale, a risk assessment, specific implemented compensating controls, a sunset date, and a rollback plan. Vague requests should be returned, not approved.
The exception registry must be queryable, actively maintained, and integrated with CI/CD — a registry that exists only as a document and is never queried at deployment time provides false assurance.

Sunset dates are only meaningful if they are automatically enforced. Automate reminders, escalations, and expiry transitions from the start; manual calendar-based enforcement reliably fails as teams and organisations change.
Exception patterns are governance signals: any architecture standard that accumulates three or more concurrent exceptions is a candidate for revision, not continued waiver.
Compensating controls must be specific, implemented (with a verifiable date), and independently verifiable. "We will be careful" is not a control.

Right-size the approval authority by risk: low-risk exceptions should complete in hours, not weeks. Uniform heavyweight review for all deviations destroys the process by incentivising teams to bypass it.
When a security incident occurs, immediately check whether any active exceptions cover the affected system and whether the incident traces to an excepted deviation.
